family_history: Sister: systemic lupus erythematosus, diagnosed age 26
No family history of epilepsy or psychotic disorders

social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, 1-2 glasses of wine on weekends
social_history.living_situation: Lives with partner in urban apartment

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert but disoriented to date and place. GCS 13 (E4 V4 M5). Intermittent periods of agitation alternating with flat affect. Unable to perform serial 7s. Immediate recall 2/3 objects, delayed recall 0/3 at 5 minutes. Intermittent echolalia and perseveration noted. Speech is dysarthric with occasional paraphasic errors.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal and reactive 3mm bilaterally. Orofacial dyskinesias present: repetitive lip smacking, tongue protrusion, and jaw movements. Intermittent left-sided facial twitching. Extraocular movements full but with saccadic intrusions. No papilledema on fundoscopy. Corneal reflexes intact. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.motor: Tone increased in all four extremities with intermittent cogwheel rigidity in upper limbs. Strength 4/5 throughout, limited by poor cooperation. Intermittent choreiform movements of both hands. Stereotypic right-hand fidgeting movements observed.
neurological_exam.sensory: Withdraws to pain in all four extremities. Light touch and proprioception difficult to assess reliably due to altered mental status and poor cooperation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 3+ bilaterally, triceps 3+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 3+ bilaterally. Patellar 3+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing inaccurate bilaterally, though limited by poor cooperation and involuntary movements. Heel-to-shin not attempted due to patient's inability to follow commands consistently.
neurological_exam.gait: Unsteady, wide-based gait with tendency to veer to the left. Requires two-person assist. Unable to perform tandem gait.
neurological_exam.additional: Autonomic instability: episodic tachycardia (HR 110-140), labile blood pressure, profuse diaphoresis. Episodes of central hypoventilation requiring supplemental oxygen observed during monitoring. Waxy flexibility noted on passive limb positioning.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive confusion, seizures, and abnormal movements over 2 weeks
patient.history_present_illness: A 31-year-old woman with no prior psychiatric or neurological history presents to the emergency department after being brought in by her partner for progressive behavioral changes and a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure at home. Approximately two weeks ago, the patient developed a flu-like illness with mild fever, headache, and malaise lasting 3-4 days, which resolved spontaneously. Within days of the viral prodrome resolving, her partner noticed personality changes including irritability, mood swings, and uncharacteristic emotional outbursts at work. She became increasingly paranoid, accusing coworkers of conspiring against her, and was reported to have had a screaming episode at school that led to her being sent home. Over the following week, she developed progressive confusion, insomnia, and difficulty with short-term memory. She began having difficulty performing basic tasks such as cooking and following television programs. Her speech became disorganized with periods of mutism alternating with incomprehensible utterances. Three days prior to presentation, her partner witnessed repetitive abnormal movements including lip smacking, tongue protrusion, and involuntary hand movements. Two days ago she had her first witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting approximately 90 seconds, which self-terminated. She has since had two additional witnessed seizures. Yesterday she became increasingly unresponsive, with intermittent agitation requiring physical restraint. Her partner reports episodes of profuse sweating and noted that her heart rate seemed rapid. She has had no recent travel, no known sick contacts beyond the initial viral illness, no tick bites, and no new medications. She has not consumed alcohol excessively or used recreational drugs. She had a normal gynecological examination three months ago.

background.symmetry: Broadly symmetric with mild right hemispheric predominance of delta activity

findings.frequency: 1-3 Hz delta activity with superimposed 12-20 Hz beta activity riding on each delta wave
findings.morphology: Rhythmic delta waves with brush-like beta superimposed, creating characteristic comb-like appearance
findings.state: Present in both wakefulness and drowsiness

findings.location: Bifrontal predominance, extending to temporal and central regions
findings.morphology: High-amplitude (100-200 µV) rhythmic delta waves, semi-continuous

findings.location: Left temporal onset with secondary generalization
findings.frequency: Rhythmic 6-7 Hz theta evolving to 2-3 Hz spike-and-wave
findings.morphology: Sharply contoured theta evolving over 45 seconds to generalized spike-and-wave complexes
findings.state: Captured during monitoring; patient appeared behaviorally unchanged (subclinical)

artifacts.description: Related to orofacial dyskinesias and choreiform limb movements; partially obscures underlying activity in brief epochs

activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: No photic driving response; no photoparoxysmal response
activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to patient's inability to cooperate

eeg.impression: Severely abnormal EEG demonstrating extreme delta brush pattern, generalized rhythmic delta activity, and one captured subclinical electrographic seizure. Findings indicate diffuse cortical dysfunction with epileptogenic potential. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Intermittent movement artifact from dyskinesias. Hyperventilation not performed.

findings.location: Bilateral medial temporal lobes, involving hippocampi and amygdalae
findings.size: Bilateral, left greater than right, approximately 2 cm in maximal anteroposterior dimension on left
findings.borders: Ill-defined, involving cortex and subcortical white matter

findings.location: Right insular cortex and frontal operculum

mri.impression: Bilateral medial temporal lobe FLAIR hyperintensities without restricted diffusion or enhancement, with additional subtle right insular cortical signal abnormality. In the clinical context of subacute encephalopathy with seizures and movement disorder, these findings are concerning for autoimmune or inflammatory encephalitis. The pattern of bilateral mesial temporal involvement favors autoimmune encephalitis over HSV encephalitis, which typically shows more asymmetric involvement with hemorrhagic change. Clinical correlation recommended.

ecg.interpretation: Sinus tachycardia with borderline QTc prolongation. No acute ischemic changes. The tachycardia is likely secondary to autonomic dysfunction and/or systemic stress response.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: WBC 11.2 x10^9/L (H), Sodium 131 mEq/L (L), Glucose 102 mg/dL (H), CRP 1.8 mg/dL (H), ESR 22 mm/hr (H), CK 185 U/L (H), Serum osmolality 268 mOsm/kg (L).

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Present (5 bands), not matched in serum — indicates intrathecal immunoglobulin synthesis
special_tests.Cytology: No malignant cells; reactive lymphocytes present

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 18 cm. WBC: 45 cells/µL (elevated) (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 72 mg/dL (elevated; normal: 15-45). Glucose: 58 mg/dL (normal; normal: 40-70) (ratio 0.57 (normal: >0.6; borderline)). Oligoclonal bands: Present (5 bands), not matched in serum — indicates intrathecal immunoglobulin synthesis. IgG index: 0.82 (elevated; normal <0.7) — supports intrathecal antibody production. HSV-1 PCR: Negative. HSV-2 PCR: Negative. VZV PCR: Negative. Enterovirus PCR: Negative. Gram stain: No organisms seen. Cytology: No malignant cells; reactive lymphocytes present. Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies: POSITIVE (titer 1:320) — diagnostic confirmation.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Anti-NMDA receptor IgG (serum) Positive (titer 1:160)  (H).

Pelvic Ultrasound Findings.value: Normal size (3.2 x 2.1 x 1.8 cm). Contains a simple follicular cyst (1.2 cm). No solid components or internal vascularity.
Mildly enlarged (4.5 x 3.0 x 2.8 cm). Contains a 2.8 cm complex cystic-solid lesion with echogenic foci suggesting calcification or fat. Internal vascularity present on Doppler.
Normal size and morphology. Endometrium 8 mm, appropriate for menstrual cycle phase.
Small amount of physiological free fluid in the pouch of Douglas

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Left ovary Mildly enlarged (4.5 x 3.0 x 2.8 cm). Contains a 2.8 cm complex cystic-solid lesion with echogenic foci suggesting calcification or fat. Internal vascularity present on Doppler.  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Left ovarian complex cystic-solid mass (2.8 cm) suspicious for teratoma

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Anti-NMDA receptor IgG (CSF) Positive (titer 1:320)  (H).

signal_characteristics.density: Mixed cystic and solid components with areas of fat density (-30 to -80 HU) and calcification
signal_characteristics.enhancement: Mild peripheral enhancement of solid component

additional_observations: Normal liver, spleen, kidneys, and adrenal glands
output.impression: No other masses or lymphadenopathy identified. No evidence of malignancy elsewhere. In a patient with confirmed anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis, this ovarian teratoma is the presumed immunogenic trigger and surgical excision is indicated. Clinical correlation recommended.

special_tests.Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies: POSITIVE (titer 1:256) — slightly decreased from initial 1:320, consistent with early treatment response

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 17 cm. WBC: 38 cells/µL (elevated, slightly decreased from initial 45) (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 68 mg/dL (elevated; normal: 15-45). Glucose: 55 mg/dL (normal; normal: 40-70) (ratio 0.55 (borderline low)). Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies: POSITIVE (titer 1:256) — slightly decreased from initial 1:320, consistent with early treatment response. Oligoclonal bands: Still present (5 bands). IgG index: 0.78 (elevated; slightly improved from 0.82).

output.query: anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis treatment protocol immunotherapy

results.title: A clinical approach to diagnosis of autoimmune encephalitis
results.authors: Graus F, Titulaer MJ, et al.
results.key_findings: Proposed diagnostic criteria for autoimmune encephalitis. First-line immunotherapy includes IV methylprednisolone (1g/day x 5 days), IVIG (0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days), or plasma exchange. Second-line: rituximab or cyclophosphamide if no improvement within 2 weeks.

results.title: Treatment and prognostic factors for long-term outcome in patients with anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis
results.authors: Titulaer MJ, McCracken L, et al.
results.key_findings: In a cohort of 577 patients, 81% had good outcome (mRS 0-2) at 24 months. Early treatment and tumor removal (when present) were associated with better outcomes. Relapses occurred in 12% of patients, more common without tumor removal or immunotherapy.

results.title: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis: clinical features and basic mechanisms
results.authors: Dalmau J, Armangué T, et al.
results.key_findings: Comprehensive review: ~80% female, median age 21. 58% have ovarian teratoma. EEG shows extreme delta brush in ~30%. Treatment algorithm: first-line (steroids, IVIG, PLEX) → second-line (rituximab, cyclophosphamide) if no improvement at 2 weeks. Tumor removal is critical when present.

output.summary: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis treatment follows a structured immunotherapy protocol. First-line therapy includes high-dose IV corticosteroids (methylprednisolone 1g/day for 3-5 days), intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days), or plasma exchange (PLEX), often used in combination. If there is no clinical improvement within 2 weeks, second-line therapy with rituximab (375 mg/m2 weekly x4) or cyclophosphamide should be initiated. Tumor removal (oophorectomy for ovarian teratoma) is essential when a tumor is identified, as it is both therapeutic and preventative of relapse. Early treatment initiation and tumor removal are the strongest predictors of favorable outcome. Long-term immunosuppression with mycophenolate or azathioprine may be needed to prevent relapse.

output.proposed: IV methylprednisolone 1g/day + IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day + levetiracetam 1000 mg BID
interactions: Methylprednisolone + oral contraceptive: Corticosteroids may reduce contraceptive efficacy; counsel patient regarding alternative contraception during treatment
IVIG + levetiracetam: No clinically significant interaction expected
warnings: High-dose methylprednisolone: Monitor blood glucose closely given stress hyperglycemia already present; risk of steroid-induced hyperglycemia and psychiatric effects
IVIG: Risk of thromboembolic events; ensure adequate hydration. Monitor renal function. Headache and aseptic meningitis are potential side effects that could confound clinical assessment
Levetiracetam: May cause psychiatric side effects (irritability, aggression) which could overlap with encephalitis symptoms; monitor closely
Hyponatremia (Na 131): Monitor sodium carefully during high-dose steroid therapy as corticosteroids can affect fluid balance
output.formulary_status: All medications available on hospital formulary
alternatives: If IVIG not tolerated: Plasma exchange (PLEX) 5-7 sessions as alternative first-line immunotherapy

If levetiracetam causes behavioral side effects: Consider lacosamide 100-200 mg BID or valproate 500 mg BID (note: valproate contraindicated in women of childbearing potential due to teratogenicity risk)
Second-line if no improvement in 2 weeks: Rituximab 375 mg/m2 weekly x4 doses

output.query: extreme delta brush EEG pattern anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis

results.title: Extreme delta brush: a unique EEG pattern in adults with anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis
results.authors: Schmitt SE, Pargeon K, et al.
results.key_findings: Extreme delta brush (EDB) was identified in 30% of adults with anti-NMDAR encephalitis. The pattern consists of rhythmic delta activity at 1-3 Hz with superimposed bursts of rhythmic beta activity at 20-30 Hz. EDB was not seen in any control subjects and appears to be unique to anti-NMDAR encephalitis.

results.title: Extreme delta brush in anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis correlates with poor outcome
results.authors: Veciana M, Becerra JL, et al.
results.key_findings: Patients with EDB had longer ICU stays and worse short-term outcomes. However, EDB presence did not affect long-term prognosis with appropriate treatment.

output.summary: Extreme delta brush (EDB) is a distinctive EEG pattern considered pathognomonic for anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis, consisting of generalized rhythmic delta activity (1-3 Hz) with superimposed beta frequency bursts (20-30 Hz). It is observed in approximately 30% of adult patients and has not been reported in other conditions. While EDB is associated with more severe disease and longer ICU stays, it does not independently predict poor long-term outcome when appropriate immunotherapy is initiated. The presence of EDB should prompt immediate testing for anti-NMDA receptor antibodies.

differential.key_distinguishing: HSV encephalitis shows temporal lobe predilection with hemorrhagic changes on MRI, positive CSF HSV PCR, and more acute febrile onset. This patient has negative HSV PCR, non-hemorrhagic bilateral temporal FLAIR changes, and prominent movement disorder/psychiatric features atypical for HSV.

Primary psychiatric disorders do not cause seizures, orofacial dyskinesias, autonomic instability, CSF pleocytosis, or extreme delta brush on EEG. This patient's neurological signs and abnormal investigations exclude a purely psychiatric diagnosis.

NMS requires prior antipsychotic exposure, presents with severe rigidity, very high CK (typically >1000 U/L), and high fever. This patient has no antipsychotic exposure, only mildly elevated CK, and the dyskinesias are choreiform rather than rigid.

CNS vasculitis typically shows multifocal strokes and vessel irregularities on angiography. MRI in this case shows no infarcts or vascular lesion pattern, and the clinical phenotype with dyskinesias and psychiatric features is more consistent with autoimmune encephalitis.

differential.diagnosis: Hashimoto encephalopathy (steroid-responsive encephalopathy associated with autoimmune thyroiditis)
differential.key_distinguishing: Hashimoto encephalopathy requires elevated anti-TPO antibodies and often thyroid dysfunction. This patient has normal thyroid function and negative anti-TPO antibodies.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to evaluate encephalopathy and seizure activity
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Extreme delta brush pattern and electrographic seizures consistent with anti-NMDAR encephalitis

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI with contrast to evaluate structural causes and characteristic findings
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral medial temporal FLAIR hyperintensities without hemorrhage or enhancement

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture with CSF analysis including cell count, protein, glucose, viral PCRs, and autoimmune antibodies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, positive anti-NMDA receptor antibodies

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, thyroid function, inflammatory markers, CK, and toxicology screen
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Hyponatremia (SIADH), mild leukocytosis, elevated CK, normal thyroid function, negative tox screen

optimal_actions.action: Obtain ECG to evaluate autonomic instability and cardiac rhythm
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Sinus tachycardia consistent with autonomic dysfunction

optimal_actions.action: Order pelvic imaging (ultrasound or CT) to screen for ovarian teratoma
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left ovarian teratoma identified as immunogenic trigger

optimal_actions.action: Initiate first-line immunotherapy (IV methylprednisolone and/or IVIG) and antiseizure medication
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Treatment plan is safe with monitoring for steroid hyperglycemia and sodium

optimal_actions.action: Refer for surgical resection of ovarian teratoma
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Tumor removal to eliminate immunogenic trigger and reduce relapse risk

critical_actions: Order anti-NMDA receptor antibody testing (serum and CSF)
Perform lumbar puncture with CSF analysis
Obtain EEG for seizure evaluation and encephalopathy characterization
Obtain brain MRI to evaluate for structural lesions and characteristic findings
Initiate first-line immunotherapy (corticosteroids and/or IVIG) as soon as diagnosis is suspected
Screen for ovarian teratoma with pelvic imaging
Start antiseizure medication for seizure control
Arrange ICU-level monitoring given autonomic instability and seizures
contraindicated_actions: Initiating antipsychotic medications without evaluating for autoimmune encephalitis — antipsychotics can worsen dyskinesias and autonomic instability in anti-NMDAR encephalitis
Discharging patient with a diagnosis of primary psychiatric disorder without neurological workup
Delaying immunotherapy pending antibody results if clinical suspicion is high
Performing brain biopsy — not indicated when clinical picture and antibodies are diagnostic
Administering empiric antibiotics for bacterial meningitis without appropriate CSF findings (CSF shows lymphocytic predominance, not neutrophilic)
key_reasoning_points: The combination of psychiatric symptoms (paranoia, personality change), seizures, orofacial dyskinesias, and autonomic instability in a young woman is the classic tetrad of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis
Extreme delta brush on EEG is considered pathognomonic for anti-NMDAR encephalitis and should immediately prompt antibody testing
Bilateral medial temporal FLAIR hyperintensities on MRI, in the absence of hemorrhage, favor autoimmune over herpetic encephalitis
CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis with elevated protein and negative viral PCRs narrows the differential to autoimmune/inflammatory etiologies
Hyponatremia from SIADH is a recognized but often overlooked feature of anti-NMDAR encephalitis
Normal thyroid function and negative anti-TPO antibodies effectively exclude Hashimoto encephalopathy
The preceding viral prodrome is characteristic — it may trigger immune dysregulation leading to antibody production
Screening for ovarian teratoma is mandatory in female patients, as tumor removal is both therapeutic and prevents relapse
Treatment should not be delayed pending final antibody results when clinical suspicion is high — empiric immunotherapy improves outcomes

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward presentation with classic clinical tetrad (psychiatric symptoms, seizures, dyskinesias, autonomic instability), pathognomonic EEG finding (extreme delta brush), characteristic MRI changes, and confirmatory CSF antibodies. Minimal confounders.
